1.synthesis: 'In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim, these kings made war with Bera king of Sodom … They also took Lot, the son of Abram's brother, who was dwelling in Sodom, and his possessions, and went their way' (vv.1–2, v.12). Genesis 14 is the most historically grounded chapter in the patriarchal narrative — four named eastern kings, five named city-state kings, a twelve-year vassal-tribute arrangement, a revolt, a punitive campaign, specific battle sites. When the four eastern kings defeat the five Canaanite kings and capture Lot, one who escaped brings the news to Abram. Abram's response is immediate: he musters his 318 trained household fighters, pursues to Dan, defeats the four kings in a night raid, and 'brought back all the possessions, and also brought back his kinsman Lot with his possessions, and the women and the people' (v.16).
1.historical_context: The four eastern kings represent the major ANE political powers of the early 2nd millennium: Shinar (Mesopotamia/Sumer), Ellasar (possibly Larsa), Elam (the power east of Mesopotamia that periodically dominated the Babylonian region), and Goiim (location uncertain). The five Canaanite city-states — Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboyim, and Bela/Zoar — had been vassal-tributaries for twelve years before rebelling. Abram's 318 'trained men born in his household' (yĕlîḏê bêtô, v.14) represents a substantial private military — a patriarch who could field 318 fighters was a significant power in the regional political landscape. The Valley of Siddim's 'tar pits' (v.10) are archaeologically consistent with the bitumen deposits known in the Dead Sea region. Abram's night raid and division of forces (v.15) is a tactically sound response to numerical disadvantage, paralleled by Gideon's night attack (Judges 7:9). The first use of 'Hebrew' (haʿiḇrî) occurs in v.13 — 'Abram the Hebrew.'
1.literary_structure: The section has three movements: the macro-campaign of the four kings (vv.1–12), Abram's rescue (vv.13–16), and then the aftermath that the next section addresses. The campaign narrative is unusually detailed for Genesis, naming kings, geography, and pre-Israelite peoples (Rephaites, Zuzites, Emites, Horites) with precision. The narrative focus narrows from the great campaign to the specific item that triggers Abram: Lot's capture (v.12). Abram's response is characterized by speed ('he led forth his trained men', v.14 — no deliberation described), faith (his 318 against four kingdoms), and comprehensiveness ('he brought back all the possessions … and the women and the people', v.16).
1.theological_application: Gill on Abram's rescue: 'Abram heard that his brother was taken captive — that is, his nephew, who was as a brother to him … he immediately took steps for his relief and deliverance, without any regard to the difficulty and danger he should meet with in engaging so powerful and victorious a confederacy.' Matthew Henry: 'Though Lot had separated from Abram, and was now in distress through his own foolish choice, Abram does not use the opportunity to let him suffer for his folly, but hastens to his relief: this was truly brotherly … He did not upbraid him with his separation from him, but helped him without reproach.' The rescue of Lot models the pattern of unreserved covenant-loyalty — going to war for the one who separated from you, recovering him at one's own risk. This is the pattern the NT identifies as love: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13).
1.christology: Abram's rescue of Lot — the kinsman-redeemer who goes to war to recover the captive — is a significant type of Christ's redemption. The 'kinsman redeemer' (gōʾēl) pattern runs through the OT (Boaz in Ruth; the legal provisions of Leviticus 25) and reaches its fulfillment in Christ who came 'to proclaim liberty to the captives' (Luke 4:18). Abram goes to war on behalf of one who chose badly — Lot chose Sodom — and recovers him completely: 'he brought back all the possessions, and also brought back … Lot' (v.16). The 'all' is emphatic: total recovery, not partial. Christ's redemption is similarly total: 'whoever comes to me I will never cast out' (John 6:37).

17.synthesis: 'After his return from the defeat of Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him … And Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine. (He was priest of God Most High.) And he blessed him and said, "Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth; and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand!" And Abram gave him a tenth of everything' (vv.17–20). The appearance of Melchizedek is one of the most theologically dense moments in the patriarchal narrative — a priest-king of Jerusalem (Salem) who predates Israel's priesthood, blesses Abram in the name of God Most High, receives the first tithe in the Bible, and attributes the military victory to divine action. Against Melchizedek's theological framing, the king of Sodom's offer ('give me the people, keep the goods') stands in stark contrast. Abram refuses any of Sodom's goods: 'lest you should say, I have made Abram rich' (v.23).
17.historical_context: Melchizedek's name means 'my king is righteousness' or 'righteous king' (malkî-ṣedeq). Salem (šālēm, peace) is identified with Jerusalem in Psalm 76:2. His appearance without genealogy — no father, no mother, no named predecessor or successor — is not necessarily supernatural but is narratively significant: the text presents him as existing outside the genealogical framework Genesis uses to locate everyone else. 'God Most High' (ʾEl ʿElyôn) is a compound divine title — ʾEl (the generic Semitic divine name) and ʿElyôn (Most High) — which Abram immediately identifies with YHWH (v.22: 'I have sworn to YHWH, God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth'). Abram's tithe of a tenth of everything (v.20) is the first tithe in the Bible, paid to a non-Israelite, non-Levitical priest — which Hebrews 7 uses to argue the superiority of the Melchizedekian order to the Levitical.
17.literary_structure: The eight-verse section sets up the contrast between two recipients of the returning Abram: Melchizedek (priestly, blessing-focused, theological) and the king of Sodom (political, transaction-focused, material). Melchizedek comes with bread and wine and speaks of God; the king of Sodom comes with an offer and speaks of goods. Abram's tithe to Melchizedek precedes and contextualizes his refusal of Sodom's goods — having already honored God Most High through Melchizedek, Abram cannot accept enrichment from any other source. The narrative structure makes Melchizedek the theological interpreter of the military victory, overriding any reading that would credit human power or political alliance.
17.theological_application: Gill on Melchizedek: 'Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine … he was a priest of God Most High, and might offer bread and wine on his altar to God, and consecrate them to him, and then give Abram a part of them in token of his blessing … This was typical of Christ, the true Melchizedek, who is both King and Priest; a King of righteousness, and a King of peace; and who has set forth bread and wine as memorials of himself, and tokens of his love to his people.' Matthew Henry: 'Melchizedek, as a priest, blessed Abram. Those whom God has blessed are blessed indeed; and God's priests, who are devoted to him, have a kind of ministerial authority to bless in his name.' On Abram's refusal (v.23): 'He would not be made rich by the king of Sodom, because he would be sure that God alone should have the glory of enriching him, and he would not throw himself under any obligation to Sodom.'
17.christology: Psalm 110:4 — 'You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' — is the oracle that Hebrews 7 expands into the most sustained Melchizedek theology in the Bible. Three features of the Melchizedek-Abram encounter are developed: (1) Melchizedek's lack of recorded genealogy types Christ's eternal priesthood, without beginning or end (Heb 7:3); (2) the greater blesses the lesser (Heb 7:7), establishing Melchizedek's superiority to Abram — and thus to Levi who was 'in the loins of Abraham' (Heb 7:10); (3) Abram's tithe to Melchizedek establishes the Melchizedekian order as recipient of the tithe, arguing Christ's priesthood is 'on the basis of indestructible life' rather than Levitical descent (Heb 7:16). The bread and wine that Melchizedek brings out (v.18) is the meal that anticipates the eucharist — the priest-king of Salem offering the elements that Christ would identify as his body and blood.
